offer.what: Nonprofit advocacy, community support, and educational programs fighting the fentanyl epidemic — including the Fentanyl Families Network (peer support + weekly trauma-informed grief sessions), Walk for Lives national events, a Thought Leadership podcast series, and policy advocacy campaigns targeting Congress
offer.coreOutcome: Turning grief into purpose: connecting bereaved families, driving congressional action, reducing fentanyl deaths, and eliminating stigma around addiction loss
offer.pricePoint: Free to join; funded by donations (no publicly disclosed giving tiers)
offer.commitment: Free network membership and event participation; recurring donation option available
leadMagnets: Newsletter signup ('Get vital updates to help educate and advocate')
Walk for Lives event registration and volunteer hub
Fentanyl Families Network membership (monthly emails + weekly grief sessions)

audience.icp: Families who have lost a loved one to fentanyl poisoning — especially parents of 18–45-year-olds — seeking community, healing, and a channel to turn grief into legislative advocacy
painPoints: Grief and isolation after losing a family member to fentanyl poisoning
Feeling powerless against an epidemic driven by supply-chain and policy failures
Stigma around addiction making loss harder to discuss publicly
Lack of local peer support networks for bereaved families
Fear for children's safety in an era of counterfeit pill proliferation
Frustration that Congress has not passed meaningful fentanyl legislation
segments: Bereaved families (lost a loved one) — core Fentanyl Families Network members
Concerned parents and community leaders — preventative advocates
Young adults and teenagers at risk — awareness and education audience
Policy advocates, lawmakers, and media — outreach and amplification targets
Corporate sponsors and organizational partners — funding and capacity

salesCycle.length: Immediate to short — free to join/sign up; donation conversion is a longer, trust-based journey
salesCycle.trial: No trial; all core resources are free (network membership, grief sessions, event participation, resource directory)
salesCycle.closeMechanism: Newsletter signup → Fentanyl Families Network membership → recurring donation ask; Walk for Lives participation → deeper volunteer/organizer engagement → advocacy spokesperson
salesCycle.topObjection: Cynicism that advocacy will produce real legislative change; or emotional inability to re-engage due to active grief and burnout

ahaMoment: Attending a weekly trauma-informed grief support session or a Walk for Lives event and realizing they are not alone — that a nationwide community of families understands their loss and is channeling it into tangible policy change

retention.recurring: Monthly curated email updates on the crisis and policy developments; weekly grief support sessions; annual Walk for Lives events (July 11 multi-city + September 26 Washington DC)
retention.expansion: Newsletter subscriber → Fentanyl Families Network member → Walk organizer/volunteer → recurring donor → national advocacy spokesperson sharing story with lawmakers
retention.churnRisk: Emotional burnout from sustained grief engagement; loss of hope that policy change is achievable; disengagement after initial crisis urgency fades

lifecycleGaps: No apparent automated Welcome & Onboarding email sequence after newsletter signup
No lead-magnet nurture converting newsletter subscribers into network members or first-time donors
No donor conversion or upgrade sequence (one-time gift → recurring giving)
No post-event follow-up sequence after Walk for Lives participation to deepen engagement
No advocacy campaign drip moving passive supporters to active congressional outreach
No win-back / re-engagement campaign for lapsed subscribers or inactive network members
toneVoice: Earnest, urgent, and deeply human — nonpartisan, community-first, emotionally resonant without being exploitative. Language centers lived experience ('grief becomes purpose'). Direct calls to action ('Demand Congress Act') paired with empathy and hope. Avoids partisan framing; emphasizes that 'fentanyl is not a left vs. right issue — it is a human issue.'
compliance: Nonprofit (501c3 assumed); sensitive audience requires careful handling of grief, loss, and addiction stigma; CAN-SPAM compliance required; no HIPAA obligation (not a healthcare provider, though grief support sessions involve vulnerable populations); mental health messaging best practices recommended; nonpartisan language must be maintained in all advocacy content
